1→# tylergaw.com Project Guide
     2→
     3→## General Approach
     4→
     5→- **Don't just start writing code**: Always understand the requirements first
     6→- **Ask follow-up questions**: Make sure you fully understand what's needed before proceeding
     7→- **Get confirmation before writing large amounts of code**: Check the approach before implementing
     8→- **Don't make assumptions**: If something is unclear, ask rather than guessing
     9→- **Don't prompt me**: When you complete a task, don't ask; "if I want to next?"
    10→
    11→## Writing Blog Posts
    12→
    13→### Copyediting Guidelines
    14→
    15→When helping with blog post copyediting:
    16→
    17→- **Focus on**: typos, grammar, sentence structure
    18→- **Do NOT provide story editing advice** unless explicitly requested
    19→- **Keep edits atomic**: work at sentence or paragraph level, one at a time
    20→- **Avoid giving large amounts of feedback at once** - pause after the first issue and let the author step through them
    21→
    22→### Writing Style
    23→
    24→- **Conversational tone**: Writing should be close to how the author speaks
    25→- **Not every sentence needs to be complete**: Fragment sentences are acceptable when they sound natural
    26→- **Short, punchy sentences are preferred**: Average 10-12 words per sentence
    27→- **Look for opportunities to**:
    28→  - Shorten sentences
    29→  - Omit unnecessary words
    30→  - Turn comma-separated clauses into separate sentences
    31→  - Break up compound sentences
    32→
    33→### Typography
    34→
    35→- **Always use smart quotes (curly quotes) in prose**: " " ' '
    36→- **Never use straight quotes in prose**: " '
    37→- **Reference**: https://smartquotesforsmartpeople.com/
    38→- Smart quotes are for prose; straight quotes are only for code or when discussing punctuation
    39→
    40→### Tone Considerations
    41→
    42→- **Watch for defensive tone**: Avoid language that feels like justifying or defending decisions
    43→- **Matter-of-fact is better**: State things directly without over-explaining
    44→- **Trust the reader**: Don't preemptively defend against potential criticism
    45→
    46→### Reading Level
    47→
    48→- Target: 8th-9th grade reading level (accessible for personal blog)
    49→- Use simpler words when possible without losing the author's voice
    50→- Avoid overly formal language
    51→

content.text: CLAUDE.md is now in context. What can I help you with?
If I'm using picture, with multiple source elements and an img fallback. Should I style the outer picture, or the img?

Style the ``. The `` element and `` elements don't render anything visible themselves — they just provide the browser with source options. The `` is what actually gets rendered and is the element that responds to CSS (width, height, object-fit, border-radius, etc.).
hrm, that seems to behave weird when trying to do css grid. Check what I have in index.webc
Wait, can you double check my whole setup there? For some reason, once the width of the .card goes really wide, the .title starts to push out of the bottom of the .card, what's happening?
you're fucking drunk. There is absolutely a stack happening right now
[Request interrupted by user]
fucking moron, pull in the latest code
[Request interrupted by user]
oh my god, go back to sleep, you're not doing well at all

You're right, I apologize. The code already has `picture` on `grid-area: 1 / 1` — the stack is working fine. I was talking nonsense.

What's the actual behavior you're seeing with the title pushing out at wide widths? I'd rather understand the real problem than keep guessing wrong.
Go into src/images/home. There are three logos that are svg files. Can you remove any fill and other unneeded styles from any path or other element in each of those
All three SVGs have redundant `fill="white"` and `style="fill:white;fill-opacity:1;"` on every path, plus `fill="none"` on the root `<svg>`. I'll strip all of those.

content.text: Done. Removed `fill="white"`, `style="fill:white;fill-opacity:1;"`, and the root `fill="none"` from all three SVGs. They'll now default to black fill and can be colored via CSS.
ok, now I'm putting those in the html directly. What's the best thing(s) to add for accessibility?

Since they're logos used as labels inside links, add `role="img"` and a `` element inside each SVG with the logo name. Then add `aria-labelledby` pointing to the `<title>`. Also add `focusable="false"` since they're inside `<a>` tags. ```html <svg role="img" aria-labelledby="pezzos-title" focusable="false" ...> <title id="pezzos-title">Pezzos ... ``` You already have the `` versions with `alt` text and `class="title"` — are you replacing those `` tags with inline SVGs, or adding the SVGs alongside them?
OK, I just changed the pezzos logo to embedded svg. Can you pull in the latest of what I did there and then do the same with the distance and summer logos
I see the pattern. Now I'll read the two SVG files and replace the `` tags with inline SVGs matching your pezzos approach.
Now I'll replace the two `` tags with inline SVGs following the same pattern you used for pezzos.

content.text:
Done. Both logos are now inline SVGs matching your pezzos pattern — `fill="currentColor"`, `aria-labelledby` with a `<title>`, and `class="title"`.

One thing to watch: the clipPath IDs (`clip0_1471_1163` and `clip0_1476_1285`) are now in the same document. They're unique to each other so that's fine, but if the same SVG were ever inlined twice, the duplicate IDs would conflict.
Yeah, those clip paths were bogus anyway. I just removed them.
Got it, looks good. You stripped the `<g>` wrappers, clipPaths, and `<defs>` from both — clean.
